preFix.title: Spinach soup with nutmeg
preFix.subtitle: Vivid green, silky, and quick
preFix.excerpt: Baby spinach wilted into a potato-thickened stock, blended with a pinch of nutmeg and a swirl of cream. A fresh, spring-leaning soup that is ready in 20 minutes and keeps its colour if eaten quickly.
preFix.sourceNotes: Spinach soup with nutmeg is a French and British classic combination: nutmeg with spinach appears in French cuisine from at least the 17th century and in British cookery writing from the 18th. Method synthesised from standard approaches.

content.text: Spinach soup follows the same rule as watercress and pea soup: cook the spinach briefly and blend immediately. Spinach needs even less time than watercress ,  1 minute at most once it hits the hot stock.

is the traditional seasoning for spinach in French and British cooking, and it works particularly well in this soup: a quarter teaspoon gives the cream a warm, aromatic quality that rounds the grassy spinach flavour.
Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a saucepan over a medium-low heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 5 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and cook for 1 minute.
Add the {{potato}} of diced potato and pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of hot stock. Bring to a simmer and cook for 10 minutes until the potato is completely tender.

Add the {{spinach}} of spinach leaves in large handfuls, pushing them down into the stock. They will wilt within 30 to 60 seconds. Blend immediately once all the spinach has wilted ,  do not simmer further.

In a jug blender, blend in batches until very smooth. Return to the pan over a low heat. Stir in {{double-cream}} of cream, {{nutmeg}} of freshly grated nutmeg, and the juice of {{lemon}} lemon. Taste and season with salt.
Ladle into bowls. Add a swirl of cream and a tiny extra pinch of nutmeg to each bowl.

items.fix: Blend as soon as the spinach has wilted ,  within 1 minute. Any longer at heat breaks down the chlorophyll. To rescue it: blend a large handful of raw spinach with a little cold water and stir into the finished soup.
items.cause: The spinach was cooked beyond wilting before blending.
items.symptom: Soup is khaki rather than vivid green

items.fix: Use a jug blender and blend for at least 60 seconds. Baby spinach is stringless; mature leaf spinach may need the stalks removed before adding.
items.cause: A stick blender cannot fully process spinach stems.

items.fix: Start with half the quantity and taste before adding more. A light hand with nutmeg is far better than an overpowering one.
items.cause: Freshly grated nutmeg is considerably stronger than pre-ground.

items.fix: The potato provides the body. If the soup is still too thin after blending, add an extra 50 g of diced potato next time and cook until very soft before adding the spinach.
items.cause: Spinach is mostly water and gives very little body on its own.

content.text: Spinach and nutmeg is one of the oldest flavour pairings in European cooking. The combination appears in French and British cookbooks from the 18th century. As a blended soup, spinach with cream and nutmeg became a British restaurant staple in the 1980s. It is simpler and faster than watercress or asparagus soup. Baby spinach is available year-round, so this soup has no season.
